finding.reachability: Any caller of simpleGet() or simpleGet.concat() that passes a user-controlled URL string or opts.url property
finding.justification: opts.url is passed directly into url.parse() and the resulting hostname/port/path are forwarded to http.request()/https.request() without any validation against private IP ranges, loopback addresses, or cloud metadata endpoints (e.g. 169.254.169.254), enabling full SSRF.

detection_pattern.description: Detects direct use of http.request/https.request or url.parse without any private-IP or allowlist guard, which enables SSRF when the URL is user-controlled.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed. In simpleGet() (index.js lines 18-22), opts.url is passed directly to url.parse() and the resulting hostname/port/protocol/auth/path fields are forwarded verbatim to http.request() or https.request() (line 49) with no validation whatsoever. There is no allowlist check, no private-IP/loopback/link-local filter (no isPrivateIP, blockPrivate, SSRF guard, or schema validation present anywhere in the codebase). The code also follows 3xx redirects recursively (lines 50-68), and the redirect target URL is again fed back into url.parse() then into a recursive simpleGet() call without any re-validation — enabling SSRF via open redirect even from an initially-safe host. The API surface is intentionally generic: simpleGet(url, cb) and simpleGet.concat(url, cb) accept a raw string URL as their first argument, the package README documents this as the primary usage pattern, and no options flag enables any guard. There is no schema validator (zod/joi/ajv) wrapping the input. The finding is reachable by any direct caller that passes a user-controlled URL string or opts.url — this is the normal, documented use case of the library. The severity is correctly rated high: full SSRF to any reachable host including loopback, RFC1918, and cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254) is possible with no bypass required.

reachability.reasoning: simpleGet is exported directly via module.exports = simpleGet (line 2) and is the primary public API of the package. All entry points (simpleGet(url, cb), simpleGet.concat(url, cb), simpleGet.get/post/put/patch/head/delete) are attached to the exported function and accept a raw URL string as their first argument — this is the documented primary usage pattern. No non-default options are required to reach the vulnerable code path. In simpleGet() (lines 18-22), opts.url is destructured via url.parse() and the resulting hostname/port/protocol/auth/path fields are forwarded verbatim to http.request() or https.request() (line 49) with no allowlist, no private-IP/loopback/link-local filter, and no schema validation anywhere in the codebase. The vulnerable parameter (the URL) is by definition user-controlled in any application that uses this library to fetch a user-supplied URL. Additionally, 3xx redirects are followed recursively (lines 50-68) and each redirect target is again fed into url.parse() then back into simpleGet() without re-validation, enabling SSRF via open redirect chains. There is no gate of any kind: no isPrivateIP, blockPrivate, SSRF guard, allowedHosts check, joi/zod/ajv schema validation, or {unsafe:true} toggle. The vulnerability is exploitable at default configuration with the library's normal, documented usage pattern.

finding.reachability: Any caller making a request to a server that can issue 3xx redirects; the attacker controls the redirect Location header
finding.justification: On 3xx responses, res.headers.location is assigned to opts.url and the recursive simpleGet() call is made without re-validating the new URL against private IP ranges, so a redirect from a public server to http://127.0.0.1/ or http://169.254.169.254/ succeeds.

detection_pattern.description: Detects redirect-following code that does not re-validate the Location header URL, allowing an attacker-controlled redirect target to reach internal services.

verdict.reasoning: The redirect handler at lines 50-68 of index.js follows 3xx redirects by assigning res.headers.location to opts.url and calling simpleGet() recursively. There is no validation of the redirect target URL against private IP ranges or any allowlist. The only guard is maxRedirects (default 10), which prevents infinite loops but not SSRF. Header stripping (cookie/authorization dropped on host change, lines 57-60) is a partial credential-leakage mitigation but does NOT block the request to internal addresses. No schema validation (zod/joi/ajv) is present. The vulnerable path requires no non-default options: any 3xx redirect is followed by default. Any caller that requests a URL from a server whose Location header can be controlled by an attacker (e.g., a server that issues 302 to http://127.0.0.1/ or http://169.254.169.254/) will have its request follow that redirect to the internal address. The reachability claim is correct: the attacker controls the redirect Location header on the intermediate server, and simple-get will follow it without re-validation.

reachability.reasoning: simpleGet is the default export of index.js (module.exports = simpleGet) and is fully public. The redirect-following behavior is enabled by default (maxRedirects: 10 is set as a default merge, not an opt-in). No non-default option is required to reach the vulnerable path: any 3xx response from any server causes res.headers.location to be assigned to opts.url and passed recursively into simpleGet() without any validation of the new URL against private IP ranges, allowlists, or schema checks. The only mitigations present are (1) maxRedirects counting (prevents infinite loops, not SSRF) and (2) dropping cookie/authorization headers on host change (prevents credential leakage to the internal target but does not block the request itself). No input validation (joi, zod, ajv, isPrivateIP, allowedRedirect) is present anywhere in the redirect handler. The vulnerable parameter (res.headers.location) is entirely attacker-controlled when the attacker can influence the HTTP server that the caller contacts. This is default-reachable with zero non-default options required.

There is no validation of the redirect target URL against private IP ranges or any allowlist. The only guard is maxRedirects (default 10), which prevents infinite loops but not SSRF. Header stripping (cookie/authorization dropped on host change, lines 57-60) is a partial credential-leakage mitigation but does NOT block the request to internal addresses. finding.reachability: Callers passing a user-controlled URL string; affected in older Node.js versions where net/http do not strip CRLF
finding.justification: The path component extracted by url.parse(opts.url) is used verbatim in the HTTP request; CRLF characters in the path (%0d%0a) can inject extra HTTP headers in Node.js versions that do not sanitize the path, enabling request smuggling or header injection.

detection_pattern.description: Detects use of raw path values from url.parse without CRLF stripping, which can allow header injection in older Node.js runtimes.

verdict.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies that simple-get v4.0.1 uses url.parse(opts.url).path verbatim at line 19 with no explicit CRLF stripping. However, two layered defenses prevent exploitation: (1) url.parse() automatically percent-encodes raw CRLF characters in the URL path (e.g., literal \r\n becomes %0D%0A), so the path value extracted from url.parse() never contains raw CR or LF bytes; (2) Node.js's http.request sends percent-encoded sequences like %0d%0a as literal text in the request line rather than decoding them to actual CRLF bytes, so the wire-level output is 'GET /test%0D%0AX-Evil:injected HTTP/1.1' with no header injection. Both behaviors were confirmed empirically on Node.js v22.22.2. Additionally, Node.js v14+ throws ERR_UNESCAPED_CHARACTERS if raw unescaped characters are passed to http.request, providing a third layer. The finding's claimed reachability is incorrect because url.parse itself neutralizes raw CRLF before the path reaches the http layer, and the http layer does not decode percent-encoded CRLF back to raw bytes. The vulnerability does not exist in any supported Node.js version (v14+) and likely never worked as described even in older versions since url.parse has always percent-encoded control characters in the path component.

finding.reachability: Any caller who passes opts.headers sourced from untrusted input (e.g. parsed JSON or user-supplied object)
finding.justification: opts.headers keys are iterated with Object.keys() and copied into a plain headers object without filtering __proto__ or constructor; an input like { "__proto__": { "admin": true } } pollutes Object.prototype via the bracket-notation assignment headers[k.toLowerCase()] = opts.headers[k].

detection_pattern.section_name: Prototype pollution via header key merge
detection_pattern.description: Detects loops that copy arbitrary keys from a user-supplied object into a plain object using bracket notation, which can pollute Object.prototype when __proto__ or constructor keys are present.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding claims that the header key copy loop at line 26 of simple-get/index.js pollutes Object.prototype via bracket-notation assignment when a key like '__proto__' is present. This claim is factually incorrect in Node.js/V8.

The vulnerable code is: Object.keys(opts.headers).forEach(k => (headers[k.toLowerCase()] = opts.headers[k]))

When k = '__proto__', this executes: headers['__proto__'] = {admin: true}

In V8 (Node.js v22.22.2 confirmed), a direct bracket-notation assignment where the key string equals '__proto__' does NOT pollute Object.prototype. It changes the [[Prototype]] of the receiving object ('headers') itself, but this does not propagate to Object.prototype or to any other object in the process. Verified empirically: after the assignment, ({}).admin is undefined and Object.prototype.admin is undefined.

True prototype pollution requires either: (1) accessing the prototype and assigning a property on it (e.g. obj.__proto__['key'] = val), or (2) a merge function that walks into nested objects and assigns on an existing prototype. The simple assignment headers['__proto__'] = val is intercepted by the JS engine as a prototype setter on the local object only.

While the headers object itself will inherit properties from the attacker-controlled value (headers.polluted would be true), this is scoped to the headers object for the duration of one HTTP request, not a global Object.prototype mutation. No other objects in the process are affected.

Additionally, there is no schema validation (zod/joi/ajv) anywhere in simple-get, and the package does accept user-supplied opts.headers. The input path is reachable. However, the claimed sink effect (Object.prototype pollution) does not occur. The finding is a false positive due to a misunderstanding of how V8 handles bracket assignment with the '__proto__' string key.

finding.reachability: Callers of simpleGet.concat() with opts.json=true making requests to attacker-controlled or compromised servers
finding.justification: JSON.parse() is called on the raw HTTP response body without any schema validation; a malicious server can return a crafted JSON payload that, when later passed to recursive merge utilities by the caller, enables prototype pollution or type confusion downstream.

detection_pattern.description: Detects JSON.parse calls on raw HTTP response data without schema validation or a reviver that blocks prototype-sensitive keys, flagging a deserialization trust boundary issue.

verdict.reasoning: The finding claims deserialization_untrusted_json at simpleGet.concat() lines 91-96, where JSON.parse(data.toString()) is called on the HTTP response body when opts.json=true, with no schema validation or reviver. The code path is confirmed: opts.json is a non-default caller-supplied option, data is the raw HTTP response body from the server, and there is no reviver, no zod/joi/ajv schema check, and no sanitizer in simple-get itself. However, the finding's claimed impact is indirect and misattributed. JSON.parse() itself does not execute code and does not cause prototype pollution on its own. The finding's own justification states the risk only materializes 'when later passed to recursive merge utilities by the caller' - meaning the vulnerable behavior is in downstream caller code, not in simple-get. The library's opts.json=true is documented, expected behavior for an HTTP client. Every HTTP client that parses JSON responses would share this 'vulnerability'. The threat model (attacker-controlled or compromised server) is inherent to any network request, and the responsibility for validating server responses against a schema lies with the caller application. simple-get does not perform any recursive merge on the parsed data - it simply returns the parsed object via the callback. No sanitizer is missing from simple-get's responsibility surface. The finding is a false positive: it correctly identifies JSON.parse without schema validation, but incorrectly assigns the risk to this library rather than to caller code that may use the result unsafely.

finding.reachability: Callers passing opts.headers with values sourced from user input
finding.justification: Header values from opts.headers are copied verbatim into the outgoing request without CRLF sanitization; in runtimes that do not enforce HTTP/1.1 header field rules, an attacker-controlled header value containing \r\n can inject arbitrary headers or split the HTTP request.

detection_pattern.description: Detects assignment of user-controlled values to HTTP header fields without stripping CRLF sequences, which enables header injection or HTTP request splitting.

verdict.reasoning: Line 26 of index.js does copy opts.headers values verbatim into the outgoing headers object without any CRLF sanitization — that code pattern is factually present. However, the claimed vulnerability (header injection / HTTP request splitting) is not exploitable in any currently supported Node.js runtime. Node.js enforces strict header value validation at the http/https module level via llhttp: passing a header value containing \r or \n throws 'Invalid character in header content' synchronously before any bytes are transmitted, regardless of whether headers are supplied as an object to http.request() options or via setHeader(). This was confirmed by direct testing on Node.js v22.22.2 (llhttp 9.3.0). The finding's own qualifier — 'in runtimes that do not enforce HTTP/1.1 header field rules' — acknowledges the dependency on a non-default runtime, but simple-get v4.0.1 uses require('http') directly and targets Node.js environments where this protection is active. There is no schema validation inside simple-get itself, but there is no sanitizer needed because the platform-level guard is present and sufficient. The finding describes a theoretical code smell, not a reachable vulnerability in any Node.js version still receiving security updates (v18, v20, v22).
